q: A compound microscope has at least two convex lenses: an objective lens and an eyepiece lens.
In a compound microscope, the lens nearer the object is called the eyepiece.
The magnifying power of a compound microscope is calculated as the sum of the magnification of the objective and the magnifying power of the eyepiece.
For a compound microscope in normal setting, the final image is formed at infinity.
When the final image is formed at a distance of 25 cm from the eyepiece, the object distance for the eyepiece is given by $u_e = \frac{25f_e}{25+f_e}$.
In a compound microscope, the focal length of the objective should be longer than that of the eyepiece.
As the magnification of a compound microscope increases, the sharpness of the images is improved.
Electron microscopes employ electron beams in place of light.
Optical microscopes have a higher maximum magnifying power than electron microscopes.
Electron microscopes use glass lenses to focus the electron beam.
The wavelength of electrons is shorter than that of light, which allows for greater magnification in electron microscopes.
The Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) is one of the two main types of electron microscope described in the text.
In a Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM), the electron beam is transmitted through the specimen.
